candidates.reasoning: The vast, treeless steppe landscape combined with distant snow-capped mountains and a high-altitude plateau appearance is highly characteristic of the Mongolian interior. The high plains or Great Basin regions of the US exhibit similar dormant grassland and scrubland biomes, though the specific soil color and mountain profile are more typical of Central Asia.

The vast, treeless high-altitude steppe combined with distant snow-capped mountains and arid, brown soil is highly characteristic of the Mongolian plateau. The extreme openness and lack of any visible vegetation other than low scrub match the Central Asian interior perfectly.

The landscape is very similar to the eastern regions of Kazakhstan where the steppe meets the Altai mountains. It is geographically compatible, though the extreme flatness of the foreground is slightly more typical of the Mongolian heartland.

This matches the landscape of Southern Siberia or the Russian Far East (e.g., Buryatia), which shares the same biome as Mongolia and Kazakhstan. The terrain is consistent with the Eurasian steppe-tundra transition.
The Patagonian steppe has a similar arid, treeless appearance with distant mountains. However, the specific soil tone and the road's asphalt quality are more typical of the Eurasian interior.

While some high-desert regions of the Western US (like Wyoming or Montana) have similar vistas, the specific combination of the wide-open steppe and the distant mountain range profile is less common than in Central Asia.
The Canadian prairies can be flat and treeless, but the soil color and the specific geological look of the distant mountains are more consistent with the Altai/Himalayan foothills than the Rockies.

The vegetation consists of low-lying, dormant xeric grasses and shrubs typical of the Central Asian steppe. This is highly compatible with the Mongolian plateau's high-altitude, semi-arid grassland biome.
The landscape is consistent with the Kazakh steppe, which shares nearly identical botanical characteristics with Mongolia, including the same types of cold-resistant, drought-tolerant grasses.
The vegetation is compatible with the southern Siberian steppes or the Altai region, where similar cold-arid grassland patterns occur.
This represents a cross-continent analog; the Patagonian steppe features similar low-growth, xeric vegetation and cold-arid conditions, though the specific grass species differ from Central Asia.

Similar botanical patterns are found in the high plains or Great Basin (e.g., sagebrush steppe), but the overall density and color of the dormant vegetation are more characteristic of the Eurasian steppe.
While the northern prairies have similar dormant grasses in winter, the lack of any coniferous fringes in this wide-open vista makes it slightly less typical than the Central Asian steppes.
